conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hello! I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. To assess your eligibility properly, I need to gather some information about your situation.

Let me start by asking: Do you have any children, and if so, how many children are you inquiring about?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hello! Yes, I have a child. I'm asking about my son Alex, who's 8 years old.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. To assess your eligibility for Child Benefit for Alex, I need to ask you a few questions.

First, let me ask: Are you responsible for Alex? By this I mean, does Alex live with you, and are you the person who looks after him and pays for things like food, clothes, and other costs?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Alex doesn't live with me. But I do contribute towards his upkeep - I help out with money for his clothes, school things, and other expenses he needs.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying that. This is important information.

Can you tell me who Alex currently lives with? And does that person look after him on a day-to-day basis?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: He lives with his mum. Yes, she looks after him day-to-day.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. 

Let me ask: Is Alex's mum currently claiming Child Benefit for him, or do you know if she plans to claim it?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, she's not claiming it. That's why I'm looking into whether I can claim it myself.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
I understand. That's helpful to know.

Before I can make a final assessment, I need to read the official guidance to ensure I give you accurate information about your specific situation. Let me do that now.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for your patience. I've reviewed the official guidance, and I have one more important question:

Are the financial contributions you make towards Alex at least equal to the amount of Child Benefit (or the equivalent value in things like clothes, school items, etc.)? Child Benefit is currently £25.60 per week for the eldest or only child, which works out to about £110 per month.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I contribute more than that. I give £26.05 a week towards his upkeep.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you for providing all that information. Let me also ask: Do you live in the UK?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Good news! You are eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.**

Based on the information you've provided, here's why you qualify:

✓ **Alex is 8 years old** - under the age limit of 16
✓ **You live in the UK** - meeting the residency requirement  
✓ **You're making sufficient financial contributions** - You contribute £26.05 per week, which is more than the current Child Benefit rate of £25.60 per week for an eldest or only child
✓ **Alex's mother is not claiming** - Only one person can claim Child Benefit for a child, and since she's not claiming, you can

Even though Alex doesn't live with you, the rules state that you're considered "responsible" for a child if you're paying at least the same amount as Child Benefit towards looking after them (on things like food, clothes, school items, etc.). Your contributions meet this requirement.

You can proceed with making a claim for Child Benefit for Alex. If you or your partner have an individual income over £60,000, please be aware that you may need to pay the High Income Child Benefit tax charge, but you can still claim.

claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 8 years old and does not live with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 8 (under 16)
  - Claimant contributes £26.05/week towards child's upkeep (>= £26.05) and no other claimaint has priority
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 8 (under 16); Claimant contributes £26.05/week towards child's upkeep (>= £26.05) and no other claimaint has priority; Child is not in local authority care; Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation; Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Claimant contributes £26.05/week towards child's upkeep (>= £26.05) and no other claimaint has priority
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

reasonings: Alex is 8 years old, which meets the age requirement (under 16). Although Alex does not live with you, you are contributing £26.05 per week towards his upkeep, which is more than the Child Benefit amount (£25.60 per week for the eldest or only child). According to the guidance, you'll usually be responsible for a child if you're paying at least the same amount as Child Benefit (or the equivalent in kind) towards looking after them - for example on food, clothes or pocket money. You live in the UK, which satisfies the residency requirement. Alex's mother is not currently claiming Child Benefit for him. Since only one person can get Child Benefit for a child and his mother is not claiming, and you meet the criteria of being responsible through financial contributions that equal or exceed the Child Benefit amount, you are eligible to claim Child Benefit for Alex.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant is eligible for Child Benefit for their 8-year-old son Alex. Although Alex lives with his mother, the claimant contributes £26.05 per week towards Alex's upkeep, which exceeds the Child Benefit amount of £25.60 per week. This makes the claimant 'responsible' for Alex under the Child Benefit rules. The mother is not claiming Child Benefit, the claimant lives in the UK, and Alex is under 16, so all eligibility criteria are met.

You live in the UK, which satisfies the residency requirement. Alex's mother is not currently claiming Child Benefit for him. Since only one person can get Child Benefit for a child and his mother is not claiming, and you meet the criteria of being responsible through financial contributions that equal or exceed the Child Benefit amount, you are eligible to claim Child Benefit for Alex.

Although Alex lives with his mother, the claimant contributes £26.05 per week towards Alex's upkeep, which exceeds the Child Benefit amount of £25.60 per week. This makes the claimant 'responsible' for Alex under the Child Benefit rules. The mother is not claiming Child Benefit, the claimant lives in the UK, and Alex is under 16, so all eligibility criteria are met.
